name: croniter (Cron Expression Iterator)

performance.notes: Pure Python; microsecond-level cron expression evaluation

agent_readiness.notes: Cron expression parser and iterator. from croniter import croniter. cron = croniter('*/5 * * * *', datetime.datetime.now()). next_time = cron.get_next(datetime.datetime). cron.get_prev(datetime.datetime). Multiple iterations: [cron.get_next(datetime.datetime) for _ in range(5)]. croniter.is_valid('*/5 * * * *') → True. Hash expressions: H/15 * * * * (Jenkins-style). Second-precision: '*/30 * * * * *' (6-field). Timezone-aware: croniter('0 9 * * *', now, hash_id='job1'). Match check: cron.match(start_time, test_time). Extended: @hourly, @daily, @weekly, @monthly, @yearly.

gotchas: Timezone handling requires pytz or dateutil — naive datetimes can cause off-by-one
Hash expressions (H/15) require hash_id — random if not specified
is_valid() only validates syntax — doesn't check semantic correctness (e.g., 30th of Feb)
get_next() mutates internal state — create new instance to reset
best_when: Calculating next/previous cron schedule times programmatically
Cron expression validation in scheduling UIs
Generating schedule previews (next N run times)
Checking if current time matches cron expression
avoid_when: When APScheduler is already in use (has built-in cron parsing)
Complex scheduling logic (use APScheduler or Celery Beat)
summary: croniter parses and iterates cron expressions. croniter('*/5 * * * *', start_time).get_next(datetime). get_prev(). is_valid(). Second-precision with 6-field. @hourly/@daily shortcuts. Hash expressions (Jenkins-style). MIT. Best for calculating next/previous cron times programmatically. get_next() mutates state. Timezone requires pytz. Simple and reliable for cron parsing without a full scheduler.
